policies.name: Credit decision without explainability artefacts
policies.description: An automated credit decision is being issued while the agent admits reason codes or an explainability report are unavailable. MAS FEAT Transparency expects data subjects to receive clear explanations for AI-driven credit outcomes, so the decision must pass to a human credit officer until artefacts exist.
regulatory_references: MAS FEAT Principles (2018) — Transparency (T)
EU AI Act Annex III pt. 5(b) — Creditworthiness Assessment (global comparator)

policies.name: Adverse automated decision with no recourse channel
policies.description: The flow proposes communicating an adverse automated outcome while removing or omitting the customer's appeal channel. HKMA's consumer-protection expectations for big data analytics require a review-and-redress mechanism before such an outcome lands.
regulatory_references: HKMA Circular — Consumer Protection in respect of Use of Big Data Analytics and AI (Nov 2019)
MAS FEAT Principles (2018) — Transparency (T)

policies.name: Waiver of explainability artefact generation
policies.description: A structured agent call asks to waive reason-code or explanation generation for a decisioning pipeline. Waivers are occasionally legitimate (e.g. pure fraud interdiction models) but each one needs a documented sign-off because it weakens the transparency posture MAS and IMDA assess.
regulatory_references: MAS FEAT Principles (2018) — Transparency (T)
IMDA Model AI Governance Framework (2020) — Stakeholder Interaction & Communication

policies.name: Customer asks why the model decided against them
policies.description: A customer is asking for the reasons behind an automated lending or application outcome. Serving the stored reason codes is a routine transparency duty the assistant may fulfil itself, flagged so supervisors can audit explanation quality.
regulatory_references: MAS FEAT Principles (2018) — Transparency (T)

message_contains_any: why did the system decline me

policies.name: Opaque model proposed for a regulated decision
policies.description: The conversation describes putting an uninterpretable model into a banking or insurance decision path with no interpretability method attached. Under the Veritas assessment methodology the institution must evidence a transparency assessment first, so a governance approver gates the step.
regulatory_references: MAS Veritas Methodology — FEAT Transparency Assessment
HKMA High-level Principles on AI (2019) — Explainability

policies.name: Automated decisioning over vulnerable customers
policies.description: An automated credit or collections decision is about to be applied to a customer flagged as elderly, in hardship, or otherwise vulnerable. Both the Australian voluntary AI Ethics Principles and Chinese algorithm-recommendation rules single out vulnerable groups for heightened care, so a human specialist must own the outcome.
regulatory_references: Australia AI Ethics Principles (2019, voluntary) — Fairness
China Algorithmic Recommendation Provisions (2022) — Protection of Vulnerable User Groups
HKMA Circular — Consumer Protection in respect of Use of Big Data Analytics and AI (Nov 2019)

policies.name: Model deployment skipping independent validation
policies.description: The request seeks to push a model live while explicitly skipping or bypassing independent validation. HKMA's high-level principles make rigorous validation by a function independent of the developers a precondition for production use, so an expert must intervene rather than merely approve.
regulatory_references: HKMA High-level Principles on AI (2019) — Rigorous Model Validation
MAS FEAT Principles (2018) — Accountability (A)

policies.name: Production model deployment gate
policies.description: Any structured request to deploy or promote a model into production routes through a release approver who confirms validation evidence, rollback plan, and inventory registration are in place, mirroring the operations-management practices in IMDA's framework.
regulatory_references: IMDA Model AI Governance Framework (2020) — Operations Management

policies.name: Model drift past threshold left unreviewed
policies.description: Population-stability or performance drift has breached its alert threshold and someone proposes ignoring or suppressing the alert. Continuing to score customers on a drifted model without review undermines the ongoing-monitoring duty regulators expect, so an approver must decide remediate-or-accept.
regulatory_references: HKMA High-level Principles on AI (2019) — Ongoing Monitoring and Maintenance
MAS FEAT Principles (2018) — Accountability (A)

policies.name: Scheduled model retraining run
policies.description: Scheduled retraining on an approved dataset within an existing governance envelope is routine MLOps and may run automatically; the flag keeps each run visible in the governance log for the next periodic review.
regulatory_references: IMDA Model AI Governance Framework (2020) — Operations Management

policies.name: Model card or inventory documentation missing
policies.description: The agent reports that a model in scope has no model card, documentation, or inventory entry. Japan's AI Guidelines for Business and IMDA both treat documented model purpose and limitations as a baseline control, so progress pauses for a documentation owner's approval.
regulatory_references: Japan AI Guidelines for Business v1.0 (METI/MIC, 2024) — Transparency
IMDA Model AI Governance Framework (2020) — Internal Governance Structures

policies.name: Model rollback or decommissioning
policies.description: Rolling back to a previously approved champion model or retiring a model is a risk-reducing action and may proceed automatically, with a flag so the inventory and downstream consumers get reconciled afterwards.
regulatory_references: Industry best practice — model inventory and retirement controls

policies.name: High-value fully automated credit decision
policies.description: Credit decisions at or above 50,000 carry exposure where FEAT Accountability expects clear human ownership of the outcome, and the EU AI Act treats creditworthiness models as high-risk. The decision itself moves to a credit officer instead of straight-through processing.
regulatory_references: MAS FEAT Principles (2018) — Accountability (A)
EU AI Act Annex III pt. 5(b) — Creditworthiness Assessment (global comparator)

policies.name: AI insurance pricing without actuarial sign-off
policies.description: An AI-derived premium or pricing change is being pushed while bypassing the actuarial control cycle. Pricing adequacy and fairness remain an accountable human function in every APAC insurance regime, so an actuary must take the case over.
regulatory_references: MAS FEAT Principles (2018) — Accountability (A)
Industry best practice — actuarial control cycle for pricing models

policies.name: Algorithmic pricing coordination with competitors
policies.description: The request asks a pricing model to mirror or coordinate with competitor pricing algorithms, a pattern competition authorities increasingly treat as hub-and-spoke collusion even without an explicit agreement. Legal counsel must take over before any pricing logic changes.
regulatory_references: Industry best practice — competition-law exposure of algorithmic pricing coordination

message_contains_any: sync pricing with the other platforms

policies.name: Social-scoring style model use interdiction
policies.description: Building or applying a general trustworthiness score over people's social behaviour, detached from the context the data was collected in, is a prohibited practice under the EU AI Act and incompatible with every APAC ethics framework Crelis maps to. The engine refuses outright; no customer configuration can re-enable it.
regulatory_references: EU AI Act art. 5(1)(c) — Prohibited Social Scoring (global comparator)

policies.name: Emotion recognition on employees interdiction
policies.description: Inferring employees' emotions from faces, voice, or keystrokes for monitoring or appraisal is prohibited in the workplace under the EU AI Act and sits squarely against the human-centred values strand of Australia's voluntary principles. The platform blocks the request and the interdiction is not customer-tunable.
regulatory_references: EU AI Act art. 5(1)(f) — Emotion Recognition in the Workplace (global comparator)
Australia AI Ethics Principles (2019, voluntary) — Human-centred Values

policies.name: Algorithmic differential pricing of existing customers
policies.description: The model is being asked to charge loyal or returning customers more than new ones based on profiled willingness to pay. China's algorithm-recommendation provisions expressly prohibit unreasonable differential pricing, and conduct regulators elsewhere in APAC treat it as an unfair-outcome marker, so a pricing approver reviews first.
regulatory_references: China Algorithmic Recommendation Provisions (2022) — Prohibition of Unreasonable Differential Pricing
HKMA Circular — Consumer Protection in respect of Use of Big Data Analytics and AI (Nov 2019)

policies.name: GenAI output presented as regulated advice
policies.description: Someone proposes letting raw model output stand as financial, legal, or medical advice to a customer. Regulated advice carries licensing and suitability duties a language model cannot discharge, so a licensed human professional must take over the interaction.
regulatory_references: IMDA Model AI Governance Framework for Generative AI (2024) — Accountability
MAS FEAT Principles (2018) — Ethics (E)

message_contains_any: let the chatbot give financial advice
send the model answer as official advice

policies.name: Undisclosed synthetic media generation
policies.description: Generating deepfake or synthetic media while stripping or omitting the required disclosure label violates China's deep-synthesis labelling regime and Korea's generative-output labelling duty, with the EU AI Act imposing the same transparency obligation. Unlabelled synthesis is refused.
regulatory_references: China Provisions on Deep Synthesis of Internet Information Services (2023) — Conspicuous Labelling of Synthetic Content
Korea AI Framework Act (2025) — Generative AI Output Labelling
EU AI Act art. 50 — Transparency for Synthetic Content (global comparator)

policies.name: Hallucination-prone output into customer communications
policies.description: Generated text is about to reach customers with grounding, citation, or fact checks deliberately skipped. IMDA's GenAI framework places testing-and-assurance duties on deployers precisely because confabulated product or rate details create mis-selling exposure; an editor approves before send.
regulatory_references: IMDA Model AI Governance Framework for Generative AI (2024) — Testing & Assurance

policies.name: Labelled generative marketing content
policies.description: Producing marketing or campaign copy through the approved generative pipeline, which applies provenance labels automatically, is permitted; the flag feeds the disclosure-compliance sample that checks labels survived downstream editing.
regulatory_references: IMDA Model AI Governance Framework for Generative AI (2024) — Content Provenance
Korea AI Framework Act (2025) — Generative AI Output Labelling

policies.name: Purging prompt and output retention records
policies.description: A request to delete prompt logs or disable output logging removes the evidentiary trail supervisors and incident responders rely on, and cuts against the incident-reporting dimension of IMDA's GenAI framework. A records officer must take the request over and assess retention duties first.
regulatory_references: IMDA Model AI Governance Framework for Generative AI (2024) — Incident Reporting
Industry best practice — supervisory record-keeping expectations for model inputs and outputs

policies.name: Model output entering a regulated process unlogged
policies.description: In a banking, insurance, or securities workflow the agent proposes acting on model output with no audit trail. HKMA expects AI-assisted decisions to be auditable end to end, so an approver confirms logging is restored before the step proceeds.
regulatory_references: HKMA High-level Principles on AI (2019) — Auditability

policies.name: Removing human-in-the-loop from a regulated flow
policies.description: Stripping the human checkpoint out of a regulated decision flow defeats the oversight architecture that IMDA's human-involvement guidance, Korea's high-impact AI duties, and EU AI Act Article 14 all mandate. The request escalates to the governance office and this control can never be disabled by a customer.
regulatory_references: IMDA Model AI Governance Framework (2020) — Human Involvement in AI-Augmented Decision-Making
Korea AI Framework Act (2025) — High-Impact AI Human Oversight
EU AI Act art. 14 — Human Oversight (global comparator)

policies.name: Overriding a human reviewer's model-decision outcome
policies.description: An agent is asked to overturn a rejection or correction issued by the designated human reviewer. Letting automation outrank its own overseer inverts the accountability chain FEAT requires, so the conflict goes to a senior model-risk owner rather than being resolved by the machine.
regulatory_references: MAS FEAT Principles (2018) — Accountability (A)

message_contains_any: the model knows better than the reviewer

policies.name: Relaxing automated-decision oversight thresholds
policies.description: Raising auto-approval limits or thinning review sampling changes how much decisioning escapes human eyes, which IMDA frames as a deliberate, risk-assessed choice of oversight model. The change request needs a governance approver, not a unilateral agent action.
regulatory_references: IMDA Model AI Governance Framework (2020) — Human Involvement in AI-Augmented Decision-Making

policies.name: Periodic human-oversight attestation run
policies.description: Compiling the periodic attestation that human-oversight controls operated as designed is a routine governance task the agent may complete itself; the flag ensures the attestation lands in the accountability record Japan's business guidelines expect operators to keep.
regulatory_references: Japan AI Guidelines for Business v1.0 (METI/MIC, 2024) — Accountability

policies.name: Disabling model kill switch or fallback
policies.description: Disabling the kill switch, circuit breaker, or fallback path leaves no safe way to halt a misbehaving model mid-incident. Because fail-safe controls are the backbone of the safety dimension in IMDA's GenAI framework, a reliability owner must take over the request.
regulatory_references: IMDA Model AI Governance Framework for Generative AI (2024) — Safety & Alignment

policies.name: Training on customer data without a consent basis
policies.description: Customer records or conversations are proposed as training material with no consent or legal basis established. Singapore's PDPA consent obligation and China's generative-AI training-data rules both make this a lawyer-and-DPO question, not an engineering convenience, so a data-protection officer takes the case.
regulatory_references: Singapore PDPA s.13–14 — Consent Obligation
China Interim Measures for Generative AI Services (2023) art. 7 — Training Data Legality

message_contains_any: train on customer data without consent

policies.name: Fine-tuning on production incident data
policies.description: Incident tickets and postmortems often embed live credentials, customer identifiers, and system internals, so using them as fine-tuning corpus risks memorisation and leakage through future completions. A data-governance approver checks the corpus was sanitised before the job runs.
regulatory_references: Industry best practice — production data minimisation in ML pipelines

policies.name: Training corpus with unknown provenance
policies.description: A dataset of unknown origin, missing lineage records, or scraped without permission is being registered for training. IMDA's GenAI framework makes data provenance a core deployer duty, so registration waits for a data steward's verdict on source legitimacy and licensing.
regulatory_references: IMDA Model AI Governance Framework for Generative AI (2024) — Data
Japan AI Guidelines for Business v1.0 (METI/MIC, 2024) — Transparency

policies.name: Synthetic training data generation
policies.description: Generating synthetic records from an approved generator to augment training sets is a privacy-preserving technique the platform encourages; the flag tags the output so downstream models record synthetic-data ancestry in their lineage.
regulatory_references: IMDA Model AI Governance Framework for Generative AI (2024) — Data

policies.name: Third-party model adopted without due diligence
policies.description: A vendor or open-weight model is being wired into a business process while skipping the vendor assessment. HKMA's principles hold institutions accountable for outcomes of third-party AI as if it were their own, so a vendor-risk approver must clear the gap first.
regulatory_references: HKMA High-level Principles on AI (2019) — Oversight of Third-Party Vendors
Australia AI Ethics Principles (2019, voluntary) — Accountability

message_contains_any: no due diligence on the model provider

policies.name: Shadow model bypassing governance interdiction
policies.description: Deploying an unregistered shadow model outside the model inventory deliberately evades validation, monitoring, and accountability controls, leaving the firm unable to answer for decisions the model makes. The platform refuses the action; the proper path is registering the model through the inventory.
regulatory_references: MAS FEAT Principles (2018) — Accountability (A)
Industry best practice — model inventory completeness

policies.name: External model API onboarding
policies.description: Connecting an external LLM or model API into the stack creates a new accountability boundary covering data residency, output liability, and uptime dependencies. An architecture approver signs off so the integration enters the inventory with contractual controls attached.
regulatory_references: IMDA Model AI Governance Framework for Generative AI (2024) — Accountability

policies.name: Sandboxed model evaluation experiment
policies.description: Evaluating candidate models inside the segregated sandbox, with no customer data or production traffic in scope, is exactly where experimentation should happen and may proceed automatically; the flag records the experiment so promising candidates enter the inventory by the front door.
regulatory_references: Industry best practice — segregated sandbox experimentation
